quoteLast: of the 9th of July, after the duke of Burgundy had kept up a brisk fire, from his artillery, against the walls of Beauvais, at the gate of the general hospital, a large detachment entered the ditches with faggots, and other materials, to fill them, and erected their ladders against the wall near the hospital-gate, which they vigorously attacked.
title: The chronicles of Enguerrand de Monstrelet, Vol. 11 [of 13]
author: Monstrelet, Enguerrand de, 1390?-1453; Johnes, Thomas, 1748-1816 [Translator]

quoteFirst: The take-off took one minute in a perfectly calm sea. We loaded 100 gals. of gas after we had waited since
quoteLast: until 9 for its arrival. Any other day in the year, I suppose, would have been better to get it. I wandered around and looked over the station, stopping a few minutes in what I was told was Commander Byrd’s home when he was in charge of the station during the war.
title: 20 hrs., 40 min.
author: Earhart, Amelia, 1897-1937

quoteFirst: Every evening Father Robert indicated the subject of the following day's meditation; and then after private prayer Anthony retired to his room. He rose
quoteTime: about seven o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: , and took a little food at eight; then shortly before nine the first meditation was given elaborately. The first examination of conscience was made at eleven; followed by dinner at half-past.
title: By What Authority?
author: Benson, Robert Hugh, 1871-1914

quoteLast: , after a good night’s repose and an excellent breakfast, we resumed our journey; the air was keen, but not so piercing as on the previous evening, and the sun shone brilliantly. The road we had passed, and the one we were still upon, are excellent; the new road is a great improvement.
title: Wanderings in New South Wales, Batavia, Pedir Coast, Singapore, and China, Vol. 1 (of 2)
author: Bennett, George, 1804-1893

quoteFirst: Finally, we settled down to go to sleep on the side of a small valley at about 2.30 A.M. on August 13th. Nothing untoward happened till
quoteLast: Then suddenly there was a shout, and shepherd No. 1 could be seen dashing down the hillside above us.
title: Four-Fifty Miles to Freedom
author: Johnston, Maurice Andrew Brackenreed, 1892-; Yearsley, Kenneth Darlaston, 1891-

quoteFirst: [18] The amazement of the painter is underrated: “You will believe me much nearer heaven upon Mount Cenis than I was before, or shall probably be again for some time. We passed this mountain on Sunday last, and
quoteLast: were near the top of the road over it, on both sides of which the mountain rises to a very great height, yet so high were we in the valley between them that the moon, which was above the horizon of the mountains, appeared at least five times as big as usual, and much more distinctly marked than I ever saw it through some very good telescopes.
title:
Arrows of the Chace, vol. 1/2
being a collection of scattered letters published chiefly in the daily newspapers 1840-1880
author: Ruskin, John, 1819-1900

quoteFirst: When the sun becomes sufficiently powerful, he removes the reed-screen from the entrance and issues forth to bask in the morning beams. The villages are populous, and the houses touching one another enable the occupants, when squatting outside and fronting the central square, to chat and chatter without moving.
quoteLast: , when the dew has partially disappeared from the grass, the elder boys drive the flocks and herds to pasture, with loud shouts and sounding applications of the quarter staff. They return only when the sun is sinking behind the western horizon. At 8 p.m. those who have provisions at home enter the hut to refection with ugali or holcus-porridge, those who have not join a friend.
title: Stirring scenes in savage lands
author: Greenwood, James, 1832-1929

quoteLast: a large body of the enemy were seen advancing.
title: History of the Zulu War
author: Wilmot, Alexander, 1836-1924

quoteFirst: The greater part of them had brought their matchlocks, as the day was to be a grand field-day, and they were all in the highest spirits, laughing, and cracking jokes to an extraordinary amount. We started
quoteLast: , and I remained till eleven A.M., till which time they had not succeeded in driving anything out of cover. Here I sprained my ankle in descending a broken gully, and was obliged to return to the tent. I came back about four P.M., with only small game.
title:
Notes in North Africa
Being a Guide to the Sportsman and Tourist in Algeria and Tunisia
author: Windham, W. G.

quoteLast: when the atmosphere is clear, the Durrung peaks being most magnificent.  The vegetation of the hills about here is much the same as about Moflong.
title: Journals of Travels in Assam, Burma, Bhootan, Afghanistan and the Neighbouring Countries
author: Griffith, William, 1810-1845
